steps.title: Identify what the question needs
steps.content: The question asks what the lice evidence, if correctly dated to 170,000 years ago, would support. The key detail: clothing-exclusive lice need large populations of clothed humans to survive — so their presence at 170Ka means regular clothing use was already established then. Compare that date to when clothing became thermally necessary (70Ka, after humans left Africa).

steps.title: Eliminate the choices
steps.content:
• Choice A — ‘it is unlikely that hominids other than anatomically modern humans produced or wore clothing even though tools enabling the production of clothing existed well before the emergence of humans.’: The passage says Africa's climate made clothing unnecessary — this reverses the logic. ✗
  • Choice B — ‘humans most likely began regularly wearing clothing at some point after 170 Ka but before approximately 70 Ka.’: The lice date to early modern humans, not other hominid species. ✗
  • Choice C — ‘a substantial proportion of humans must have regularly worn clothing long before humans permanently inhabited areas cold enough to necessitate doing so.’: 170Ka predates the 70Ka out-of-Africa migration by 100,000 years, meaning clothing existed well before survival demanded it. ✓
  • Choice D — ‘the spread of regular clothing use among humans after approximately 70 Ka may have been motivated by factors other than the thermal benefits that clothing provides.’: The lice evidence precedes 70Ka by a wide margin, so cold climate cannot explain the origin. ✗
That confirms C is correct.
